coredata.dc:title: Clozapine-laden carbon dots delivered to the brain via an intranasal pathway: Synthesis, characterization, ex vivo, and in vivo studies
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Patel, Hetal P.
Desai, Priya H.
Patel, Rikin V.
Lodha, Sandesh R.
Gore, Anil H.
Patil, Pravin O.
Desai, Bhargavi V.
Desai, Ditixa T.
Vyas, Bhavin A.
Willcox, Mark D.P.
Maulvi, Furqan A.
coredata.dc:description: Clozapine, which is widely used to treat schizophrenia, shows low bioavailability due to poor solubility and high first-pass metabolism. The study aimed to design clozapine-loaded carbon dots (CDs) to enhance availability of the clozapine to the brain via intranasal pathway. The CDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid and urea at 200 °C by hydrothermal technique and characterized by photoluminescence, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS), and Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR). The optimized clozapine-loaded CDs (CLZ-CDs-1:3–200) showed a quasi-spherical shape (9–12 nm) with stable blue fluorescence. The CDs showed high drug solubilization capacity (1.5 mg drug in 1 mg/ml CDs) with strong electrostatic interaction with clozapine (drug loading efficiency = 94.74%). The ex vivo release study performed using nasal goat mucosa showed sustained release of clozapine (43.89%) from CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 for 30 h. The ciliotoxicity study (histopathology) confirmed no toxicity to the nasal mucosal tissues using CDs. In the rat model (in vivo pharmacokinetic study), when CDs were administrated by the intranasal route, a significantly higher concentration of clozapine in the brain tissue (Cmax = 58.07 ± 5.36 μg/g and AUCt (µg/h*g) = 105.76 ± 12.31) was noted within a short time (tmax = 1 h) compared to clozapine suspension administered by intravenous route (Cmax = 20.99 ± 3.91 μg/g, AUC t (µg/h*g) = 56.89 ± 12.31, and tmax = 4 h). The high value of drug targeting efficiency (DTE, 486%) index and direct transport percentage (DTP, 58%) indicates the direct entry of clozapine-CDs in the brain via the olfactory route. In conclusion, designed CDs demonstrated a promising dosage form for targeted nose-to-brain delivery of clozapine for the effective treatment of schizophrenia.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: CAUL institutions 2023: Hybrid journals full funding
coredata.dcterms:subject: Clozapine
Carbon dots
Schizophrenia
Brain targeting
Pharmacokinetic study

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formulation of clozapine-CDs complex and drug loading efficiency study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopy and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) images of clozapine-loaded carbon dots (CLZ-CDs-1:3–200).

Ex vivo release study of clozapine from CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 dispersion (equivalent to 3 mg clozapine) and clozapine suspension (3 mg) using nasal mucosa in stimulated nasal fluid (n=3, mean ± standard deviation). Insite shows histopathological images (×100) of control nasal mucosa and nasal mucosa exposed to CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 dispersion for 30 h.
(A) Concentration-time profiles of clozapine up to 6 h post-dosing in Plasma (A) and Brain (B) following intranasal and intravenous administration in albino rats (n=3, mean ± standard deviation).
Pharmacokinetic parameters of clozapine in brain and plasma after administration of clozapine solution via intravenous route and CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 via intranasal route.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: * = Parameters estimated using mean concentration-time profiles obtained from different rats per time point (n=3). @ indicates that the units for Cmax is µg/ml and AUCt is µg/h*ml. $ indicates that the units for Cmax is µg/g and AUCt is µg/h*g.

head.ce:title: Clozapine-laden carbon dots delivered to the brain via an intranasal pathway: Synthesis, characterization, ex vivo, and in vivo studies

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Hetal P. Patel and Furqan A. Maulvi have made equal contributions as corresponding authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Clozapine, which is widely used to treat schizophrenia, shows low bioavailability due to poor solubility and high first-pass metabolism. The study aimed to design clozapine-loaded carbon dots (CDs) to enhance availability of the clozapine to the brain via intranasal pathway. The CDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid and urea at 200 °C by hydrothermal technique and characterized by photoluminescence, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS), and Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR). The optimized clozapine-loaded CDs (CLZ-CDs-1:3–200) showed a quasi-spherical shape (9–12 nm) with stable blue fluorescence. The CDs showed high drug solubilization capacity (1.5 mg drug in 1 mg/ml CDs) with strong electrostatic interaction with clozapine (drug loading efficiency = 94.74%). The ex vivo release study performed using nasal goat mucosa showed sustained release of clozapine (43.89%) from CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 for 30 h. The ciliotoxicity study (histopathology) confirmed no toxicity to the nasal mucosal tissues using CDs. In the rat model (in vivo pharmacokinetic study), when CDs were administrated by the intranasal route, a significantly higher concentration of clozapine in the brain tissue (Cmax = 58.07 ± 5.36 μg/g and AUCt (µg/h*g) = 105.76 ± 12.31) was noted within a short time (tmax = 1 h) compared to clozapine suspension administered by intravenous route (Cmax = 20.99 ± 3.91 μg/g, AUC t (µg/h*g) = 56.89 ± 12.31, and tmax = 4 h). The high value of drug targeting efficiency (DTE, 486%) index and direct transport percentage (DTP, 58%) indicates the direct entry of clozapine-CDs in the brain via the olfactory route. In conclusion, designed CDs demonstrated a promising dosage form for targeted nose-to-brain delivery of clozapine for the effective treatment of schizophrenia.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Clozapine (CLZ) is an antipsychotic drug used in treatment-resistant schizophrenia, which affects > 23 million individuals worldwide [1,2]. It belongs to biopharmaceutics classification (BCS) class II molecules which show poor aqueous solubility (lipophilic drug, log P = 3.23), low dissolution rate, gastrointestinal degradation, high first-pass metabolism, and subsequently low oral bioavailability of < 27% [3,4]. The patients discontinue oral therapy due to gastric hypomotility, hepatotoxicity, and cardio-toxicity [5,6]. Therefore, to overcome the above issues associated with high-dose oral drug delivery, we explored the nose-to-brain route to deliver clozapine using carbon dots. This is the first attempt to use carbon dots to deliver clozapine intranasally.

A non-invasive intranasal route has been extensively studied by researchers for brain targeting [7–9]. As a dependable and convenient method of administering medications systemically, the intranasal route has attracted considerable attention over the past decade [10,11]. This approach permits brain targeting, overcomes the blood-brain barrier, avoids hepatic first-pass metabolism, allows non-invasive delivery of drugs, and improves bioavailability [12,13]. Abdulla et al., 2021 formulated a nanoemulsion based in situ gel for intranasal delivery of clozapine and noted sustained release for 24 h [14]. Velupula et al., 2020 developed a mucoadhesive microemulsion of clozapine using oleic acid, Labrasol and Transcutol P [15]. The drug targeting efficiency (DTE = 3.73) and % DTP (nose to brain transport, 78.45%) was found to be significantly high compared to drug solution administered orally. Sayed et al., 2021 fabricated mixed micelles of clozapine labeled with Technetium-99 m [16]. Ex vivo data showed sustained release up to 24 h with a high brain/blood ratio in animal studies. The recent clozapine nanoemulsion sol-gel system fabricated by Tan et al., 2022, showed 8 h sustained release in an ex vivo study [17]. In our previous work, we reduced the size of clozapine and stabilized using α-tocopherol polyethylene glycol 1000 succinate and noted a 3.56-fold improvement in brain drug concentration [18]. In another study, clozapine was encapsulated in a nanostructured lipid carrier and a 6.25-fold increase in relative bioavailability was observed through the intranasal route [19].

The present study is the first attempt to use carbon dots (CDs) as nanocarriers to deliver clozapine intranasally. CDs are attractive nano-agent (5–10 nm) with superb biocompatibility, physical stability, and synthetic flexibility with tailored functional groups [20]. A high surface-to-volume ratio improves the drug loading capacity and promotes high permeability for nose-to-brain delivery [21]. CDs are considered to be a promising strategy for drug delivery without any modification to the drug molecule [22,23]. They can provide fluorescence at the site of application so localization of the drug to the site can be easily proved. Li et. al., 2018 formulated polyethyleneimine-modified fluorescent CDs as a vaccine delivery system for intranasal immunization and observed a bio-imaging function to trace antigen transport [24]. Hopkins et al., 2014 demonstrated the transport of QDs (CdSe/ZnS nanocrystals) from the olfactory tract to the brain via inhalation [21].

In this work, CDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid and urea by hydrothermal technique and characterized by photoluminescence, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS), and Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) and quantum yield. 1.5 mg/ml clozapine was loaded onto the optimized CDs and adherence of the drug to the CDs was ascertained by dialysis method, UV spectroscopy, FTIR spectroscopy, XRD study and TEM images. The ex vivo study using goat nasal mucosa and details pharmacokinetic studies using a rat model was performed on the optimized clozapine-loaded CDs to determine its intranasal efficacy to deliver clozapine to the brain tissue.

Clozapine was gifted by Sun Pharma (Gujarat, India). Urea, sodium phosphate dibasic dihydroxide, and potassium dibasic dihydroxide were purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai, India). Citric acid and sodium chloride were obtained from Central Drug House Pvt. Ltd. (Mumbai, India) and SD Fine-Chem. Ltd. (Mumbai, India) respectively. Ultra-pure water (type I, 18.2 MΩ) was obtained using a Synergy U. V. Millipore Water Purification System. All other reagents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich chemicals (St Louis, MO, USA) and used without further processing.

Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal technique using citric acid and urea (Table S1) as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively [25,26]. Different ratios of citric acid to urea were investigated, and varying temperatures were employed to study their impact on the particle size, fluorescence intensity, and stability of the CDs [27]. The required amounts of urea and citric acid were dissolved in 15 ml of deionized water and transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel thermal reactor with a 30 ml capacity. The reactor was operated at different temperatures as detailed in Table S1 for a duration of 14 hours to promote the formation of CDs. After cooling the thermal reactor to room temperature, the aqueous CDs dispersion was collected and subsequently purified for impurities and other large aggregates using a dialysis membrane (HIMEDIA, molecular weight cut-off of 500 Da) for 4 h. The purified aqueous solution of CDs was then subjected to freeze-drying (Bioassat, India) to obtain dry solid CDs, ensuring the preservation of the CDs' structural integrity and optical properties [28].

The optical properties of the CDs were investigated using UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy (UV-3092, Shimadzu, Japan) at 253 nm. The fluorescence spectra were obtained in standard 10 mm fluorescence quartz cells and recorded at 337 nm (λex) using photoluminescence spectroscopy (RF 6000, Shimadzu, Japan) [26]. The fluorescence spectra of the CDs of batch prepared at 1:3 ratio (200 °C) were also measured at three different dilutions (10 μg/ml, 100 μg/ml and 1000 μg/ml). The shape and size of CDs were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI's Tecnai™ G2 F30, Japan) at the accelerating voltage of 300 kV [29,30]. Elemental analysis of the selected CDs batch was performed by Thermo Scientific X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (Escalab 250Xi, USA) using a monochromatic Al-K X-ray source (h = 1486.68 eV) [31,32]. Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FTIR) of the selected CDs batch was recorded (500–4000 cm−1) to identify the presence of the functional groups on the surface of CDs nanoparticles (FTIR-4100, JASCO, Japan) [33]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) pattern was recorded using a Siemens Diffractometer D5000 with Ni-filtered monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (Wavelength = 1.5406 A°) [34,35]. The diffraction pattern was recorded in the interval of 10–40° diffraction angle (2θ) in a step scan mode of 1° per step/ minute at the voltage of 40 kV and 25 mA current (D8 QUEST, Bruker, USA). The quantum yield of the optimized CDs sample was determined by comparing the UV–vis absorbance values and the integrated fluorescence intensities (λex = 360 nm) of the CDs solution with those of the quinine sulfate solution [33,36]. CDs solution was suitably diluted using type I water to obtain a UV absorption of less than 0.1 (to reduce reabsorption). The calculation equation of quantum yield is as follows (quinine sulfate, QY = 0.55):
Φ x =
Φ
ST
Grad
x
Grad
ST
η
2
x
η
2
ST

The subscript ‘ST’ represents CDs sample and the subscript ‘X’ represent standard quinine sulfate. The’ Φ’, ‘Grad’ and ‘η’ are refereed to fluorescence quantum yield, gradient from the plot (integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance) and reflective index of solvent respectively. To ensure linearity over the measured concentration range, a gradient is used instead of simply dividing integrated fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation of clozapine-CDs complex and drug loading efficiency study
ce:section.ce:para: Clozapine (5 mg/ml fixed level) was interacted with CDs (selected CLZ-CDs-1:3–200) at five different concentrations to determine the required level of CDs to load the target drug (>1.5 mg/ml). 1 ml of clozapine (5 mg/ml in 10% methanolic solution) was mixed with 1 ml of CDs at varying concentrations (1–5 mg/ml) and stirred using an incubator with a shaker (100 rpm) at 37 °C overnight. The resultant dispersions were filtered separately using a 0.22 µm microfiltration membrane to remove unreacted clozapine (as solid particles above the membrane). The resultant complex of CLZ-CDs in the filtrate was quantified for clozapine content using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-3092, Lab India, Mumbai).

The selected CDs (CLZ-CDs-1:3–200) dispersion (1.5 mg drug in 1 mg/ml CDs) was dialyzed against 10 ml of deionized water through a dialysis tubing (HIMEDIA 500 K MWCO) for 48 h [37]. The amount of drug diffused from the dialysis tube (CLZfree, free drug) was analyzed using a UV-Visible spectrophotometer. The drug adherence efficiency (DAE) was calculated using the equation:
DAE
%
=
CLZ
t
−
CLZ
free
/
CLZ
free
× 100 %
CLZt denotes the total drug loaded and CLZfree refers to the amount of drug dialyzed into the deionized water.

The UV spectra of clozapine, CLZ-CDs and CDs (10 µg/ml) were observed in the range of 200–400 nm using a UV-Visible spectrophotometer. FTIR, TEM and XRD studies of CLZ-CDs were performed as discussed in Section 2.3.

The ex vivo release study was performed using Franz diffusion cell and fresh goat nasal mucosa (Maniyar slaughter house, Gujarat). The mucosal membrane (without the septum part) was cleaned using saline solution to remove the adhered tissues and clamped between the donor and receptor compartment (equilibrium time 30 min) [38]. The receiver compartment was filled with 10% methanolic stimulated nasal fluid (50 ml, pH 5.5) and 2 ml of CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 dispersion (equivalent to 3 mg clozapine) or clozapine suspension (3 mg) was placed in the donor compartment. The temperature was maintained at 37 ± 1 °C under continuous stirring (50 rpm). 2 ml sample was withdrawn at predetermined time intervals (up to 30 h) and replaced with an equal volume of media. The samples were quantified for clozapine using HPLC at 260 nm [39]. The experiment was repeated three times and results were recorded as mean ± standard deviation.

Ciliotoxicity studies were performed on the goat nasal mucosa from the test group (24 h after treatment with CLZ-CDs-1:3–200, equivalent to 3 mg clozapine) and the control groups (no treatment and negative control). The nasal mucosa samples were trimmed, rinsed with saline and treated with 10% formalin solution (overnight). The tissues were cut using a microtome (5 µm thickness) and subjected to hematoxylin-eosin staining[40]. The stained slides were observed under a light microscope to detect any nasal injury.

The animal study was approved (Protocol MPC/IAEC/13/2021) and performed as per the guidelines of the Institutional Animal Ethical Committee of Maliba Pharmacy College (Gujarat, India). The comparative pharmacokinetic study was performed using albino rats (either sex) weighing 250–350 g, housed under standard conditions (12 h light/dark cycles) in an animal house. The animals were divided into two groups (n=21 for each group and three rats for each time point) [41]. In the first group, rats received 0.1 ml of optimized CLZ-CDs (equivalent to 150 µg clozapine) via intranasal route using a micropipette. In the second group, rats received clozapine suspension (equivalent to 150 µg clozapine) via tail (parenteral route) using a syringe. At predefined time points post-administration (0.15, 0.3, 1, 2, 4, 6, and 24 h) the rats were sacrificed under light anesthesia and the blood was immediately collected in heparinized tubes. Blood samples were centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C and the plasma supernatants were quantified for clozapine using HPLC.

The intact brain was removed, carefully washed with physiological saline (to remove blood residues) and blotted dry using blotting paper. The brain (after weighing) sample was homogenized in solvent (phosphate buffer pH 7.4 and methanol 1:1 ratio) using a tissue homogenizer (Ika, Ultra-Turrax T25, Germany) at 1500 rpm for 15 min. Brain homogenates were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C and the resultant supernatant was quantified for clozapine using HPLC. The graphs of clozapine concentration versus time for the intranasal and parenteral routes were plotted [42]. The pharmacokinetic parameters like AUC (area under the curve), Cmax and tmax were determined using PK-solver software v2.0 (both for plasma and brain). The drug targeting efficiency (DTE) index represents the brain-to-plasma partitioning ratio of the drug administered by the intranasal route compared to a parenteral route.
DTE ( % ) =
(
AUC
brain − IN
/
AUC
plasma − IN
)
( AUC
brain − IV
/
AUC
plasma − IV
)
X 100

The AUCbrain-IN and AUCplasma-IN represent AUCt calculated in brain and plasma respectively after intranasal administration, whereas, AUCbrain-IV and AUCplasma-IV represent after intravenous administration. The value of DTE > 100% confirmed that the preferential drug transport to the brain is via the intranasal route compared to systemic administration [43].

To calculate the clozapine fraction that is transported via olfactory and trigeminal nerve pathways, the direct transport percentage (DTP) was determined by subtracting the contribution of the indirect pathway (i.e., absorption into the systemic circulation) from the total AUCbrain-IN:
DTP ( % ) =
AUC
brain − IN
−
AUC
brain − IV
AUC
plasma − IV
X
AUC
plasma − IN
AUC
brain − IN
X 100

Thus, the DTP value > 0 (the smaller the ratio of AUCbrain-IV: AUCplasma-IV, the larger the DTP value) suggest efficient brain targeting through a direct nose-to-brain pathway. The value from -∞to 0 indicates brain targeting by the IV route [41,44].

The pharmacokinetic parameters like AUC (area under the curve), Cmax and tmax were determined using PK-solver software v2.0. The data are shown as mean ± standard deviation. At each time point, a statistical comparison was performed between IN and IV groups using an unpaired two-tailed student’s t-test. Differences were considered statistically significant for p-values lower than 0.05.

The particle morphology of the CDs was observed by TEM (shown in supplementary material Fig. S1). The CDs displayed well-dispersed quasi-spherical morphology and the size increased with an increase in the level of urea. The size of CDs batches synthesized at 1:1, 1:3, and 1:5 ratio (citric acid: urea) was 3–5, 9–12, and 18–25 nm respectively (Table S1). A similar increment in CDs size at higher urea concentration was noted by Zholobak et. al. 2016 [45]. In the UV chamber, all CDs showed blue fluorescence (Fig. S2) at 253 nm and the intensity increases with ratio (i.e., an increase in the level of urea) due to an increase in intermolecular H-bonds (surface passivation by urea on CDs) [46–48]. The CDs batches were stored (stability study) at room temperature for three months. In the case of CDs prepared at 1:1 ratio, the fluorescence intensity disappeared (did not obey the Beer Lamberts law) within two days (Fig. S3). While, at 1:5 ratio, the solution appeared crystalline after 15 days. Thus, we selected the CDs batch at 1:3 ratio as it showed consistent blue fluorescence intensity for three months. In another study, the CDs batches at 1:3 ratio were prepared at three different temperatures. The TEM data (Fig. S4) showed CDs size in the range of 45–52, 18–23, and 9–12 nm for 160°C, 180°C, and 200°C batches respectively (Table S1). Therefore, 200°C temperature and 1:3 ratio of citric acid: urea was selected to develop the optimized batch for further studies.

The optical behavior of the optimized carbon dots (CDs-1:3–200) was investigated by the UV–visible absorption spectrum (Fig. S5) which shows a characteristic peak at around 337 nm (n-π* of -CO or -CN) indicating the presence of oxygen and nitrogen functional groups. The intensity of the fluorescence (selected CDs batch CDs-1:3–200) was also determined at three different dilutions (Fig S6, 10 µg/ml, 100 µg/ml, and 1000 µg/ml). The emission maxima at 440 nm (λex = 337 nm, excitation wavelength) decrease with an increase in the concentration of CDs due to collisional quenching and self-absorption [49]. As discussed, the HR-TEM image of optimized CDs-1:3–200 showed a quasi-spherical shape with a diameter ranging from 9 to 12 nm (Fig S1), indicating the carbon walls possibly consist of > 20 graphene layers [50].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to investigate the composition (surface group) and the valance state of optimized CDs-1:3–200. The XPS survey spectrum of the carbon dots (CDs) depicted in Fig. S7 displays distinctive peaks representing the carbon (C1s) and oxygen (O1s) elements at binding energies of 284 eV and 532 eV, respectively. Additionally, a smaller peak at 400.5 eV signifies the presence of nitrogen (N1s) in the CDs [50,51] and is associated with substitutionally doped nitrogen in the graphitic bonding configuration [52]. The composition of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen elements was found to be 76.4%, 22.7%, and 0.9%, respectively. The C1s XPS spectra (Fig. S8) can be fitted into four carbon species: sp2 C (CC/C-C) at 284.91 eV, sp3C (C-N) at 285.76 eV, sp3C (C-C, C-H) at 284.29 eV, and -CO at 286.60 eV (referring to different bounding types to C atoms) [53]. The high resolution deconvoluted XPS spectra of N1s resulting in three distinct peaks at binding energies of 398.66 eV, 400.11 eV and 401.38 eV, which corresponds to the C-N, N-H, and N-O peaks, respectively [47]. The spectrum of O1s contains two high-resolution XPS spectra that are associated with C-O at 531.59 eV and CO at 533.19 eV (sp2 hybridization).

The XRD pattern of CDs-1:3–200 (Fig S9) showed a remarkably sharp peak at 21.98° (dominant) indicating a well-ordered highly crystalline structure (d-spacing value of 4.03 Å) [29]. The FTIR spectra of CDs-1:3–200 show characteristic peaks at 3317 cm−1 (symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching of -NH and -OH), 1448 cm−1 (-CH bending vibration), and 1021 cm−1 (-CN stretching vibration) [54,55]. The quantum yield of the optimized CDs (CDs-1:3–200) batch was noted to be 21.15% [56]. The data indicates the successful production of CDs for therapeutic applications.

To investigate the drug loading efficiency, CDs (CLZ-CDs-1:3–200) at five different concentrations (1–5 mg/ml CDs in water) interacted with fixed strength of clozapine (5 mg/ml). As expected, the solubility of clozapine in presence of CDs dispersion was increased from 0.24 ± 0.12 mg/ml to 1.54 ± 1.34 mg/ml (Fig. S10), which indicates molecular dispersion/ doping of clozapine on the surface of CDs (high surface area). Thus, the strength of CDs was selected as 1 mg/ml with 1.54 mg/ml clozapine loading (target clozapine loading).

The selected CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 dispersion (1.5 mg drug in 1 mg/ml CDs) was dialyzed against 10 ml of deionized water through a dialysis tubing and the amount of clozapine adhered to CDs was found to be 94.74%, which confirmed strong interaction of drug with CDs.

The TEM and SAED (selected area electron diffraction) images of clozapine-loaded carbon dots (CLZ-CDs-1:3–200) are shown in Fig. 1. The image showed amorphous (as per SAED pattern) quasi-spherical particles (8–11 nm) without aggregation [57]. In UV–visible absorption study, the spectrum of clozapine and CDs showed characteristic peaks at 260 nm and 337 nm respectively (Fig. S11). While, the clozapine peak in CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 was shifted to 272 nm indicating the attachment of clozapine on CDs [58].

The FTIR spectra of clozapine, CDs and CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 are shown in Fig. S12 to S14. Clozapine showed characteristic bands at 1592 cm−1 and 1552 cm−1 (CN stretching vibrations), 1453 cm−1 (aromatic CC starching vibrations) and 816 cm−1 (C-Cl stretching) [59]. The FTIR spectra of CDs-1:3–200 show characteristic peak at 3317 cm−1 (symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching of -NH and -OH), 1448 cm−1 (-CH bending vibration), and 1021 cm−1 (-CN stretching vibration). While, the FTIR spectra of CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 showed broadening of peak at 3297 cm−1, which indicates hydrogen bounding between clozapine and CDs. The presence of adsorbed water layer on CDs is confirmed by a peak at 1636 cm−1 (stretching vibrational mode of water) [60]. In XRD study (Fig. S8), clozapine showed sharp crystallinity peaks at 10.5°, 17.4°, 19.4°, and 23.7° [61]. However, the XRD pattern of clozapine loaded CDs (CLZ-CDs) did not showed sharp peaks of clozapine suggesting molecular dispersion of clozapine (amorphous nature) on the surface of CDs. The d-spacing value of CDs plates were increased from 4.03 Å to 4.03 Å, which indicate the doping of drug molecules in the CDs structure.

The ex vivo release of clozapine from the CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 in 10% methanolic stimulated nasal fluid (pH 5.5) was compared with control group (clozapine suspension). The release profile of control group showed high clozapine release (98% release within 10 h), while slow and sustained release of clozapine was noted with CDs group ( Fig. 2). CDs batch showed 43.89% drug release after 30 h, which indicates strong electrostatic interaction of CDs with clozapine or it could be due to accumulation of drug-CDs in the tissue. However, it is difficult to predict in vivo intranasal performance of formulations based on ex vivo drug release data, due to lack of mimicking mucociliary clearance [62].

The ciliotoxicity study of goat nasal mucosa was conducted to assess the potential toxic effects of clozapine-loaded CDs on nasal mucosa integrity. The histopathological images (Fig. 2) from the control group, which comprised goat nasal mucosa receiving no treatment as the negative control, did not show any indications of nasociliary damage, necrosis, or signs of inflammation such as erythema or edema. The nasal mucosa, inclusive of cilia and epithelial linings, remained intact. The epithelium of the nasal mucosa treated with CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 (equivalent to 3 mg of clozapine) exhibited no significant alterations in the cilia or nasal epithelium mucosa linings. These findings suggest that the formulation was well-tolerated and safe for nasal administration. However, in a few slides, damage and detachment of the epithelial lining were observed, likely the result of prolonged lack of blood and nutrient supply, leading to tissue damage [17]. Overall, the ciliotoxicity study indicated that the CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 batch did not have a significant harmful effect on goat nasal mucosa.

The mean plasma and brain concentration versus time profiles of clozapine after intranasal and intravenous administration of optimized CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 are shown in Fig. 3. The pharmacokinetic parameters [Cmax, tmax and AUC (Area under curve)] were estimated by non-compartment model analysis ( Table 1). A significant difference (p < 0.01) in Cmax, tmax and AUC was noted in brain and plasma after intranasal administration compared to intravenous route. The CDs dispersion administered by intranasal route showed significantly higher concentration of clozapine in brain (Cmax = 58.07 ± 5.36 μg/g) within short time period (tmax = 1 h) compared to clozapine suspension administered by intravenous route (Cmax = 20.99 ± 3.91 μg/g and tmax = 4 h). The data support the hypothesis that intranasal administration of CDs via intranasal route can provide faster onset of action with even low dose. The high value of DTE (486%), suggest high clozapine accumulation in brain tissue after intranasal administration compared to intravenous route. The level of clozapine in plasma at all-time points was found to be lower with intranasal route compared to intravenous route, which was expected as intravenous route delivers drug directly in the blood.

The drug administered by intravenous route needs to cross blood brain barrier (BBB) to reach the brain tissues, while, two different pathways are involved in intranasal route. After intranasal administration, drug can directly enter brain via olfactory route and trigeminal pathways and/or nose to systemic circulation and then traveling through BBB to reach brain. Hopkins et al., 2014 reported transport of CDs from nose to brain via microtubules-mediated axonal transport (intracellular) [21]. CDs were picked by olfactory sensory neurons to olfactory bulb rather than by trigeminal nerve or nasal cavity. In our study, the high value of DTP (58.43%) with CDs after intranasal route support the theory of direct entry of drug-CDs to the brain via olfactory route. Thus, the high surface area (size < 10 nm) of aqueous CDs system proved to be an effective carrier for direct nose-to-brain delivery rather than nose-to-blood-to-brain delivery.

In present work, efficient nose-to-brain delivery of clozapine was demonstrated using carbon dots (CLZ-CDs-1:3–200) prepared at 1:3 ratio of citric acid to urea at 200 °C by hydrothermal technique. The CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 showed consistent blue fluorescence, quasi-spherical shape (9–12 nm), high clozapine loading efficiency (94.74%), and strong electrostatic interaction with clozapine. In ex vivo release study using goat nasal mucosa, CLZ-CDs-1:3–200 showed slow and sustained release of clozapine (43.89% drug release after 30 h) without ciliotoxicity. The in vivo pharmacokinetic study in rats showed a significantly high level (DTE = 486%) of clozapine after administration of CLZ-CDs intranasally compared to clozapine suspension administered by intravenous route. The high value of DTP (58.43%) with intranasal route supports the theory of direct entry of drug-CDs in the brain via olfactory route bypassing BBB, hepatic first-pass metabolism, systemic drug dilution effects, and thereby reducing drug delivery to non-target tissues (minimizing toxicity). In conclusion, clozapine-loaded CDs demonstrated a promising dosage form for brain-targeted delivery via intranasal route to treat schizophrenia.

Clozapine-loaded CDs can be further optimized based on drug loading rate and/or drug delivery efficiency to minimize the total amount of CDs required for the final formulation. Conducting a comparative study with other innovative drug delivery systems, such as nanoemulsions, liposomes, or nanoparticles, could evaluate the superiority of CLZ-CDs in terms of brain targeting efficiency and drug release kinetics. Dose optimization studies could determine the optimal concentration of clozapine-loaded carbon dots required to achieve a therapeutic concentration in the brain while minimizing potential systemic side effects. Additional studies on clozapine distribution in different parts of the brain, its receptor binding affinity, neurotransmitter modulation, and behavioral outcomes in animal models of schizophrenia can provide insights into its tissue distribution, mechanism of action, and potential therapeutic benefits. Long-term efficacy and safety studies of CLZ-CDs can offer valuable insights into the sustained therapeutic effect and potential adverse effects of intranasally delivered medication. Lastly, the translation of preclinical findings into clinical trials could validate the efficacy, safety, and feasibility of intranasal delivery of clozapine-loaded carbon dots in human subjects.

Ditixa T. Desai: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Investigation. Bhavin A. Vyas: Visualization, Software, Resources, Project administration. Pravin O. Patil: Methodology, Investigation. Bhargavi V. Desai: Validation, Software. Furqan Maulvi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Mark D.P. Willcox: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Priya H. Desai: Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hetal P. Patel: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Resources, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Rikin V. Patel: Investigation. Sandesh R. Lodha: Investigation. Anil H. Gore: Investigation.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2024.113862.
